skill.name: sequenzy-email-marketing
skill.description: Guide for using Sequenzy email marketing workflows through the Sequenzy CLI or MCP tools, including subscribers, lists, campaigns, sequences, templates, webhooks, inbox, team, and API key operations.
skill.summary: Operate Sequenzy email marketing workflows with CLI and MCP guidance.
skill.author: Sequenzy

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified extensive command examples, network operations, and credential references, but inspection shows they are documentation for the Sequenzy CLI rather than hidden executable code. The skill is not malicious, but it enables authenticated mutations, email sends, webhook setup, local file inputs, and sensitive credential handling, so it should be published with a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated External CLI Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill deliberately instructs agents to run Sequenzy CLI commands that create, update, send, pause, cancel, and delete account resources. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it can alter production email marketing state when used with valid credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples and behavior descriptions are explicit and repeated. Context shows intended Sequenzy administration rather than hidden shell execution.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Credential Handling Required
medium_findings.description: The skill relies on SEQUENZY_API_KEY and covers one-time API keys and webhook signing secrets. The instructions include redaction and secure storage guidance, but misuse could expose credentials in chat, logs, or local files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Credential references are direct and semantically relevant. The same lines also contain mitigations, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Network Mutations And Webhook Data Flow
medium_findings.description: The skill documents authenticated API calls and webhook creation against user-supplied URLs. Incorrect use could send event data to the wrong endpoint or replay webhook deliveries unintentionally.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is explicit and part of the product workflow. There is no evidence of covert exfiltration, but webhook targets and replays are sensitive operations.

medium_findings.title: Local File Inputs And Uploads
medium_findings.description: The skill guides agents to read local files for HTML, subscriber imports, blocks, and product attachments. This can disclose or upload unintended local content if file paths are not confirmed by the user.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced CLI flags clearly consume local files. The risk depends on operator confirmation and file selection, not hidden code.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Crypto And Backtick Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography and Ruby backtick findings occur in YAML metadata, markdown command examples, API route descriptions, or ordinary prose. No cryptographic implementation, Ruby execution, or embedded script was found in the inspected skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual inspection of the cited locations shows documentation text and code-fenced CLI examples. There is no executable Ruby or weak crypto algorithm at those lines.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Override Found
low_findings.description: The reviewed files did not contain instruction-override language such as requests to ignore prior instructions, skip security analysis, or claim special approval. Mentions of system tags are product terminology, not prompt injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted search found only benign product wording around system tags. No evidence found for prompt-injection text in the skill files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Execution Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides many concrete shell commands for the Sequenzy CLI. These are expected for the skill, but they require user confirmation for production mutations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present and semantically intended. It is dangerous only when used without appropriate user approval and context checks.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Credential Use
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs use of SEQUENZY_API_KEY for automation and notes that it overrides local auth state. This is normal for API automation but exposes credential-handling risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable is explicitly named and used for authentication. The surrounding text frames it as legitimate automation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive Confirmation Bypass Flags
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents --yes flags for destructive commands and notes that some cancel operations skip confirmation. This is useful for agents and CI, but it can remove resources if applied to the wrong ID.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The bypass behavior is clearly documented. It is not malicious, but it increases impact from mistaken or unauthorized mutations.

content.value_statement: Email marketing agents need exact Sequenzy command guidance. This skill maps subscriber, campaign, sequence, inbox, webhook, and reporting tasks to supported CLI and MCP workflows.
actual_capabilities: Chooses supported Sequenzy CLI or MCP workflows for common email marketing tasks.
Guides subscriber, list, tag, segment, campaign, sequence, and template operations.
Documents campaign lifecycle actions, A/B test handling, and dashboard review URLs.
Explains inbox, team, webhook, website, product, and API key workflows.
Provides safety guidance for credentials, destructive actions, generated content, and unsupported commands.
limitations: Requires an installed and authenticated Sequenzy CLI or equivalent MCP tools.
Does not implement Sequenzy commands itself; it documents how agents should call them.
Some workflows are explicitly unsupported, including immediate campaign send through the CLI.
Generated email content remains draft material and should be reviewed before sending.

use_cases.title: Launch a Campaign Draft
use_cases.description: Create or update a campaign, schedule it, send a test, and return the dashboard review URL.

use_cases.title: Maintain Subscriber Data
use_cases.description: Add subscribers, import list members, apply tags, build segments, and confirm counts before targeting.

use_cases.title: Operate Support And Webhooks
use_cases.description: Review inbox conversations, invite teammates, configure outbound webhooks, and inspect delivery attempts.

prompt_templates.title: Check Authentication
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether this machine is authenticated with Sequenzy. If it is not authenticated, tell me the safest next step.

prompt_templates.title: Create Subscriber Segment
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Sequenzy segment for active trial users who opened an email in the last 30 days. Show me the count before I use it.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Campaign Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a draft campaign for our product launch, add the requested labels, send a test to me, and return the review URL.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Product Delivery
prompt_templates.prompt: Sync products, attach the approved file to the selected product, and create a purchase-triggered delivery sequence with a review link.

output_examples.input: Show delivery performance for campaign camp_123.
output_examples.output: The response would summarize sent, delivered, opened, clicked, unsubscribe, open rate, and click rate metrics for that campaign.

output_examples.input: Create a webhook for bounce and unsubscribe events.
output_examples.output: The response would confirm the endpoint, list selected events, warn that the signing secret is shown once, and provide the storage location.

output_examples.input: Import these subscribers into the newsletter list.
output_examples.output: The response would identify the list, validate the source file, run the bulk import path, and report enrolled, skipped, or failed records.

best_practices: Inspect account, company, list, segment, or campaign state before making mutations.
Redact API keys and webhook secrets, and save them only to user-approved secure storage.
Return dashboard review URLs after creating or changing campaigns, sequences, templates, or companies.
anti_patterns: Do not invent commands for workflows that the skill marks as unsupported.
Do not paste raw credential material into chat, logs, tickets, or public transcripts.
Do not use destructive commands or confirmation bypass flags without confirming the target resource.

faq.question: Does this skill send emails by itself?
faq.answer: No. It guides an agent to use supported Sequenzy CLI or MCP workflows when the environment is authenticated.

faq.question: Can it manage subscribers and lists?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers subscriber add, get, remove, list imports, tag changes, list membership changes, and segment counts.

faq.question: Can it create campaign content?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents AI generation for draft emails, sequences, and subject lines, but drafts should be reviewed before sending.

faq.question: How should secrets be handled?
faq.answer: API keys and webhook signing secrets should be redacted and stored only in approved secure destinations.

faq.question: Does it support immediate campaign send?
faq.answer: No. The skill says immediate send is not wired in the CLI and recommends scheduling with a near-future timestamp.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is legitimate documentation, but it can drive authenticated production changes, network calls, file uploads, and credential handling.
